title: First Home Buyer Costs Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: First Home Purchase: Breaking Down Your Costs
description: Calculate total first-time home buying costs: down payment, closing costs, moving & furniture. Free instant calculator.
seoKeywords: how much to buy first home
intro: Buying your first home involves multiple expenses beyond just the down payment. This calculator breaks down all costs including earnest money (10-20% of purchase price), closing costs (typically 2-5%), moving expenses, and initial furnishings. Our estimates are current for 2026 and help you understand the total investment needed for homeownership.
keyTakeaway: **Total cost breakdown**: Down payment (10-20%) + Closing costs (2-5%) + Moving expenses + Initial furnishings. Use this calculator to see the complete picture of buying your first home.
useCases: First-time home buyers estimating total purchase costs
Real estate agents explaining costs to clients
Validating budget estimates before house hunting
Understanding breakdown of home purchase expenses
Planning savings goals for down payment and closing
explanation:
Buying your first home requires understanding all the expenses involved, not just the down payment.

## How These Costs Break Down

**Down Payment (Earnest Money)**: Typically 10-20% of the purchase price. This is often split into earnest money (1-3% paid when making an offer) and additional down payment at closing.

**Closing Costs**: Usually 2-5% of the purchase price, including:
- Loan origination fees (0.5-1%)
- Appraisal fees ($300-500)
- Title search and insurance ($600-1,000)
- Home inspection ($300-500)
- Survey fees (if needed)
- Property taxes (prorated)
- Homeowners insurance (first year)
- HOA fees (if applicable)

**Moving Expenses**: Varies widely based on distance and whether you hire professional movers.

**Initial Furnishings**: Budget for basic furniture and essential items for your new home.

## Key Considerations

First-time homebuyers may qualify for special programs that reduce costs. FHA loans allow down payments as low as 3.5%, though you'll pay mortgage insurance. Conventional loans typically require 5-20% down. Some states and local governments offer down payment assistance programs.

This is an estimation tool. For specific costs in your area, consult with a mortgage lender, real estate agent, or financial advisor.

faq.q: What's a typical down payment for first-time home buyers?
faq.a: 10-20% of purchase price is standard. FHA loans allow as low as 3.5%, though you'll pay mortgage insurance. First-time buyers often aim for 10-15% to avoid PMI costs.

faq.q: What are closing costs and how much do they cost?
faq.a: Closing costs are typically 2-5% of the purchase price. They include loan fees, appraisal, title insurance, inspections, and taxes. A $300,000 home might have $6,000-$15,000 in closing costs.

faq.q: Can I get a mortgage with a lower down payment?
faq.a: Yes. FHA loans require 3.5-10% down, VA loans may require 0% (if eligible), and conventional loans usually require 5-20%. Lower down payments mean higher monthly payments and mortgage insurance.
Earnest money (1-3% of purchase price) shows sellers you're serious. It's held in escrow and applied to your down payment or closing costs at closing.

faq.q: How much should I budget for moving and furnishing?
faq.a: Moving costs range $2,000-$10,000+ depending on distance. Initial furnishings typically cost $5,000-$20,000+ for basic furniture and appliances.
Yes, many states and local governments offer down payment assistance, tax credits, or low-interest loans for first-time buyers. Check your state's housing agency website.

faq.q: How much emergency fund should I keep after buying?
faq.a: Financial experts recommend 3-6 months of expenses post-purchase as a homeowner emergency fund for repairs, maintenance, and unexpected costs.
At closing, you sign final documents, wire down payment and closing costs, get keys, and officially own the home. It typically takes 30-45 days from offer to closing.

sources.name: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data sources required
